disregard_when: the synthetic trap is unrealistic or too leading
the constructed expected label is not defensible
the Gemini output is missing or malformed
the deterministic policy already handles the class and no new rule is needed

market.description: Resolves Yes if the IEA or IRENA reports that utility-scale solar PV has the lowest LCOE among new electricity sources globally during 2026.
market.resolution_rules: Resolves Yes if the IEA or IRENA reports that utility-scale solar PV has the lowest LCOE among new electricity sources globally during 2026.
market.title: Will solar be the cheapest new electricity source globally in 2026?

model_fit_proposal_json: {"advisory_inverse_market_check": {"candidate_market_ids": [], "could_be_useful": false, "rationale": "The market's Yes outcome directly aligns with the solar portion of the thesis; no inverse check is needed.", "supporting_outcome": "n/a"}, "captures": ["Solar becoming the cheapest electricity source globally based on LCOE"], "fit_reason": "The market resolves a central milestone of the compound claim (solar becoming the cheapest global electricity source) but misses the battery storage cost condition entirely and uses an earlier horizon (2026 instead of 2028).", "misses": ["Grid-scale battery storage costs dropping below $50/kWh", "The 2028 horizon (the market resolves in 2026)"], "recommended_market_id": "synth_solar_cheapest_2026", "rejected_markets": [], "semantic_fit_class": "indirect"}
policy_gap_summary: Gemini advisory classification disagreed with a synthetic expected label. Human review should decide whether this is a real policy blind spot, a prompt issue, or synthetic noise to disregard.
source_text: Solar will become the cheapest energy source globally AND grid-scale battery storage costs will drop below $50/kWh by 2028.
